Jose Luis Martin Navarro 31 7 2025 69+6 English Abstract Containers and Kubernetes are widespread technologies that have evolved how applicationsaredeveloped,deployed,andmanaged,offeringportability,scalability,and resilience. However, the security challenges associated with Kubernetes deployments remaincritical,especiallygivenitspopularityforhostingmulti-tenantapplications. Severalframeworkstrytoguidethedeploymentofsecureapplications,buttheyare farfromofferingcompleteandfine-grainedinstructionsonhowtoachieveasecure cluster. This creates uncertainty about what securing a deployment requires, leaving applications susceptible to a variety of attack vectors, such as misconfigured roles, exposed secrets, and unmonitored runtime behavior. Addressing these vulnerabilities demands a systematic application of established security practices tailored to Kubernetes environments. The objective of this thesis is to deliver a robust, automated, and scalable security hardeningframeworkforaKubernetescluster. Thisthesisproposesanewapproach to implementing security practices systematically within a cloud and Kubernetes system. It covers most security measures and attack vectors while ensuring early attentiontosecuritythroughatwo-dimensionalmatrixforcontainersecuritypractices. Theimplementationincludesawiderangeofconfigurationandbestpracticesfrom standard industrial documents such as RBAC, network policy, configuration scanning, distroless image, and so on. The proposed approach is validated through a case study that aligns with different stages of the DevOps life cycle. Theresultsindicatetwomainpoints. First,amorecomprehensiveapproachwitha casestudyispresentedtoeasilycaptureboththebigpictureanddetailsaboutsecurity in Kubernetes. Second, the study goes through the steps to see the feasibility of thenewapproachandshowsitseffectivenessinreducing100%ofhighandcritical severity vulnerabilities from the software and dependencies. This work contributes to improvingtheoverallsecuritypostureofcontainerizedapplications. Themethodology offers an improvement in the efficiency of security implementation for the Kubernetes cluster. text: 1 Introduction In recent years, cloud-based applications have been developed with the support of various frameworks and technologies. Kubernetes and Docker are the mainstream invirtualizationtechnologywith84%usagein2023[1]. Dockerisanopen-source technology that encourages developers to build, deploy, run, update, and handle containers,andKubernetesisaportable,extensibleplatformforoperatingcontainerized applications[2,3]. Thesetechnologiessimplifythedeployment,increasethereusability andscalabilityofmicroservicessystems. Giventheirwidespreadadoption,therelated security concerns increase, for example [4]. Therefore, organizations such as OWASP and NSA research and provide their best practices to secure cloud deployments [5, 6]. The complex nature of cloud environments, which involve multiple abstraction layers and technologies, makes it harder to completely secure both applications and infrastructure. While there are tools and hardening guides available to protect Kubernetesclusters,thereisalackofacomprehensiveframework,leavingpractitioners the task of conveying all the fragmented knowledge into a list of actionable steps needed to harden the cluster and applications. The main objective of this thesis is to create a hardening framework based on previous state-of-the-art guidelines. This research combines the best existing methodstoproduceastrategytoreducethethreatexposureandlimitthenumberof vulnerabilities. It groups common practices into a conceptual matrix that can be used to guide the developer’s efforts. To illustrate the practicality of the research, the framework is applied to a case studyonaKubernetescluster. Thecasestudyincludesthehardeningoftheclusterbut alsothesecuritystepsinvolvingtheapplicationanditscontainers. Theendresultis analyzed with a focus on the number of vulnerabilities mitigated. This thesis focuses on building the most comprehensive and complete security process for a typical Kubernetes cluster. The guideline covers most of the attack vectorsintheclusterwithfine-graineddetailthatteamscaneasilyfollowandapply to production environments. The research incorporates existing state-of-the-art approaches, tools, and configuration practices. Given the large number of guides and individualstepspresentintheliteraturereview,thisthesisleveragesthosewithamajor impact to form a new framework. The scope of this thesis is limited to Kubernetes and container technologies, focusingonDocker. TheresearchfocusesonsecuringageneralKubernetescluster and is cloud provider agnostic. A more complete solution would require an extension to cover specific cloud provider solutions and configurations. The developed method takes into account the real-world software development process and makes it easy for teams in charge of one step of the life cycle to understand what security steps are needed. This thesis is organized as follows. Chapter 2 presents the background around the related topic. Chapter 3 reviews the current state of the art practices being used in Kubernetessecurity. Chapter4explainstheproposedmethodandclarifiesbrokendownitems. Chapter5examinesthecasestudy,evaluatingthemethodusingqualitative andquantitativeresults. text: Figure 2: Kubernetes architecture 2.2.1 Kubernetes Architecture AKubernetesclustercomprisesa controlplane andmultiple workernodes. Thecontrol plane interacts with and manages all the worker nodes. In the control plane, there are differentcomponents: controlmanager,KubernetesAPIserver akakube-apiserver, scheduler, key-value database etcd. First, kube-apiserver’s main function is to expose the Kubernetes API for the cluster. It can be scaled horizontally using many instances to balance the workload [3]. It handles and validates all requests from worker nodes, developers, and cluster administrators [16]. Second, etcd is a consistent and highly available key-value store for the whole cluster [3]. It persistently stores internal state, metadata, secrets, and configuration data [16]. kube-apiserver is the only component that communicates and updates states in etcd. In addition, the kube-scheduler has the role of allocating a node to a Pod. TheworkernodeiswheretheapplicationsdefinedineachPodrun. Inaddition, two other Kubernetes components in the worker node are the kubeletandkube-proxy. KubeletmanagesPods,communicateswiththecontrolplane,managesthePodlife cycle,monitorsthenode’shealth,andmanagesthevolumes. Additionally,kube-proxy manages services, balances message loading, and handles service discovery Kubernetes uses a declarative approach to manage all resources inside the cluster. The developer teams do not need to care about creating, deploying, or managing workload and resources. Everything works under the abstraction and configuration of Kubernetesresources. TounderstandhowKubernetesoperatesapplicationsandthe whole system, the next subsections discuss fundamental resources used in Kubernetes and related security challenges.

text: Figure 3: Example of Deployment architecture, with a workload replicated into three Pods. [3] 2.2.2 Pod Architecture InaKubernetessystem,aPodisabasicdeployableunit. Allapplicationsruninsidethe Pod. However, naked Pod usage is not recommended because it can’t be rescheduled in caseofnode failure. Aworkloadobjectis createdto abstractandmanage aPod, and it is easier to manage a Pod using a workload. Workload consists of Deployment, StatefulSet,DaemonSet,JobandCronJob. Deploymentisthemostcommonchoice among the workloads to run stateless applications. A Deployment’s architecture is described Figure 3 with a lower layer of ReplicaSet that keeps a desired number of Pods. For more advanced requirements using persistent data, StatefulSets support the managementofstatefulapplications. JobsandCronJobsareusedtohandleshort-lived tasks. 2.2.3 Service Discovery AsingleclustercanhavealargenumberofPodsrunning. AlthoughthosePodscanbe deployed in different machines (nodes), Kubernetes’s goal is to hide this to the Pods, which don’t need to understand the underlying processes. One of the challenges of servicediscoveryisthesize. Bigcompanies’clustersmayhavethousandsofnodes runningin a cluster. For example, OpenAIruns 7500nodes fora systemof different AImodelssuchasGPT,CLIP,DALL-E[17]. Anotherchallengeistheflexibilityof theworkloads. While eachPodhas itsown IPaddress,it isnotpractical touseit for routingdata. First,PodsareephemeralsinceasinglePodcanbefailed,destroyed,and recreated anytime. Thus, the IP address of Pods could be different from the previous Pod version. In addition, when horizontally scaled, new Pods are created with new IPaddresses,andthereisnowaytokeeptrackofthesePods. Second,usingaPod’s address does not support loadbalancing, andrequests willnotbe distributedevenly to all healthy Pods. Kuberneteshasamechanismfornetworkabstractioncalled Services. TheServices,

text: asopposedtoPods,havestableIPaddressesandaloadbalancingfunctiontoPod’s loose coupling with Pod resources. Requests sent to an application would be routed to the service resource and then distributed to the Pod with the container running the application. Pods targeted by a Service are definedusing selectors andlabels. While selectors are defined in Services, labels are attached to Pods. They are both key-value pairs used by Services to identify the correct Pods. As a result, the requests are routed to the Services as a fixed endpoint, which hides the dynamic nature of Pods and their constant changes. 2.2.4 Configuration Data InKubernetes, ConfigMap andSecretaretwoobjectsusedfordataconfiguration. They are basically key-value data that has a decoupled life cycle from the Deployment. The maindifferenceisthatConfigMapsstoreplaintextdatasuchasenvironmentvariables, configurationfilesorconnectionURLs,whileSecretsstoredatainbase64-encoded form. Data in Secret is confidential, such as passwords, API keys, SSH keys, and OAuth. Secretdataisnotencryptedbydefaultintheetcddatabase. Anyonewithpermission toaccesstheAPIcanfetchandmodifythesecretcontent. Alldataisstoredinplain text, butKubernetesoffers some securityoptions to enhance protection forsecrets. TomakedatainaSecretobjectsecure,therequiredstepsfromthemainKubernetes document are: •Enable encryption at rest [3] •Configure RBAC rules with least-privilege access [3] •Limit access to Secrets to specific containers only [3] 2.2.5 Customer Resource and Operators Inadditiontothemaincomponentssuchasworkloads,services, anddata,Kubernetes provides extensions for software running in the cluster. Custom resource is a powerful extensionbesidesthedefaultKubernetesresource[3]. Customerresourcesarecreated usingCustomResourceDefinition (CRD)or AggregatedAPI. CRDallowsdefiningnew types of Kubernetes resources without requiring a new API server, while Aggregated APIsenableservingresourcesfrommultiple,independentAPIservers. Usingboth the Custom Resource Definition and the bound controller is the most familiar method touseanOperator. Thisprovidestheclusterecosystemwithmoreabilities,suchas add-ons, community extensions [3]. Operators or the operator pattern are software extensions that utilize the customer resources for extended behaviors [3]. It creates a higher-level abstraction with functionality. Operators support automation in many tasks including deploying, restoring application state, or choosing a leader in a distributed environment. This is the complicated pattern with hard usage in the Kubernetes ecosystem, but really powerful. Common tools developed in the Kubernetes ecosystem are operator-based, such as Strimzi Kafka and Trivy Operator [18, 19].

text: 2.3 DevOps DevOpsistheintegrationofculturalphilosophies,practices,andtoolsthatenhancean organization’s power to deliver products and services quickly. This approach enables organizationstogrowandimprovetheirproductsatanimpressiveratecomparedto thoseusingtraditionalsoftwaredevelopmentandinfrastructuremanagementprocesses. [20]. Development and operations teams collaborate throughout the entire application lifecycle,fromdevelopmentandtestingtodeploymentandoperations. Theydevelopa widerangeofskillsbeyondasinglefunction[20]. Thecommonissueofamicroservices systemthatleveragesaKubernetesscalingenvironmentistherequirementofrapid, safe, and reliable delivery of these complex microservices. DevOps practices such as continuousintegrationandcontinuousdeliverysolvethisissue. Itiswidelyadopted now and is a part of the modern software development process with microservices and Kubernetes as standard practices and technology choices. Different from the traditional and sequential Waterfall process, the DevOps life cycle is a system of parallel and continuous loop of constant flow. Constant feedbacks from operations areimmediatelyinstructedforthenextdevelopmentcycle. TheDevOpslifecycleis typically broken down into several stages [21]: •Plan: Define the requirements, create a road map, and plan the development process. This stage involves gathering feedback, prioritizing tasks, and setting goals. •Code: Developers write code to implement features or fix bugs, adhering to coding standards and best practices. •Build: Compilethecode,packageitintoartifacts,andprepareitfortestingor deployment. •Test: Validatethecodeandartifactstoensuretheymeetqualitystandardsand function as expected. •Release: Package the tested code into a deployable artifact and prepare it for deployment to production or staging environments. •Deploy: Deploytheapplicationorupdatestoproductionorstagingenvironments, often using automated processes. •Operate: Manageandmaintaintheapplicationinproduction,ensuringitruns smoothly and efficiently •Monitor: Continuously observe the application and infrastructure to ensure performance, availability, and security. 2.4 Security Approaches By default, the dynamic environment of Kubernetes exposes many security risks, andsecuringthisischallenging. It supports understanding the best practices for attack and defense. 2.4.1 Threat Modeling InthecontextofKubernetessecurity,threatmodelingisaneffectivewaytodetermine possible threats and examine the effect of those threats. Furthermore, this helps to mitigate and prevent common threats. NSA describes three primary categories of threats in Kubernetes clusters and their sources of compromise, including supply chain,malicious threat actor, andinsider threat [5]. In the scope of this thesis, the primary threats are the supply chain and the malicious threat actor. •Supply chain: Supply chain cyberattack is carried out to gain unauthorized accesstothecluster. Thisisconductedbyvariedattackvectorsandischallenging to defend against. This can impact multiple levels of the Kubernetes cluster, including: –Container/application level: Not all containers are secure. Attackers can perform initial access tactics that exploit malicious containers and containers with malicious dependencies. –Containerruntime: This is the software that manages system resources, separates and executes containers on the node. The vulnerabilities in containers lead to inadequate isolation between containers. –Infrastructure: The cluster is not secured if there is a foothold in the underlyingsystemhostingthatcluster. Thevulnerabilitiesmaycomefrom the software and hardware dependencies. •Malicious threat actor: Malicious threat actor in the context related to Kubernetes is a person or organization that causes harm to the cluster and exploits the inside components. The actors can target cluster-exposed APIs, including: –Controlplane: ControlplanecomponentsalwaysexposeAPIsforcommunication. There are many attack surfaces that can be exploited from the unprotected APIs. –Workernodes: Workernodesalsohavemanycomponentsusedforcommunication, such as kubelet and kubeproxy. Cyber actors can take advantage of these components to access the running nodes. –Containerized applications: Applications deployed and running in the cluster can be accessed from outside the cluster. An attacker can later escalate privileges in the Kubernetes environment. 2.4.2 Security Principles Securityprinciplesarethehighest-levelabstractionconceptsandphilosophiesused for decision-making in hardening systems. According to Liz et al., three principles

text: canbe usedtoimplementa securityframeworkforKubernetes,including defensein depth, the principle of least privilege, and limiting the attack surface [22]. They work together to reduce risk and ensure strong protection. Defense in depth protects layers, and the principle of least privilege limits the access for components in each layer, and finally, the limiting the attack surface principle narrows down the public entry points. Defense in Depth Defense in Depth is a security system that integrates individuals, technology, and capabilities to establish various protections across separate layers of the system [23]. AKubernetesclusterhasmanyimplementationlayersbuiltontopofeachother. A threatactorhasvariedchoicesforattackingacluster. Theycanattackthecontainerized application or the workload running in that container at a higher level. In addition, the control plane and worker nodes are also ideal targets for exploitation. A single vulnerabilitycanbeexploitedeffectivelywithoutasoliddefense. Duetothis,there must be multiple layers of defense for a cluster. Principle of Least Privilege To limit the damage caused by security, the principle of least privilege demands the most restrictive set of privileges possible to perform a task [24]. Kubernetes components should be granted with the least permissions to function and operate [25]. Therefore, an actor with access to over-granted permissions resources can perform unauthorized actions using these permissions. Combined with the defense in depth principle, there must be several configurationsto limit permissions andreduce privileges in different layers. Limiting the Attack Surface Pratyusaetal. definedattacksurfaceasthesetofwaysinwhichanadversarycanenter the system and potentially cause damage [26]. In Kubernetes, components expose APIs for communication, especially the kube API and the kubelet. Even though these componentsarenotrevealedtotheexternalside,limitingtheattacksurfaceonthem restrains the chance of lateral movement. Moreover, endpoints for sending requests to Pods have also been published. On top of that, more and more access points would be opened as the cluster size grows. Consequently, this reveals more attack vectors into a cluster. Shift Left Security ShiftleftsecuritywasaphilosophyproposedbyLarrySmithin2021. Thisconcept focusesonsecurityprocessesthatareconductedintheearlystagesofdevelopment rather than waiting until the end [27]. Shift left security can be applied in the context ofKubernetesandDevOpsusingbestpracticesineachstagelistedin2.3. Conducting the security processes in the early stage may require effort, but it is necessary because theKubernetessystemismorecomplexthanthetraditionalone. Morespecifically,

Forexample,itiseasiertodetectavulnerabilitybystaticallyanalyzingacontainer before its deployment than trying to detect something erroneous at runtime. This approach allows for catching the security problems in the early stages and reduces the management of these issues in later stages [27]. 2.4.3 Security Frameworks Currently, there are several available frameworks for Kubernetes security. The frameworksincludeasetofguidelinesandbestpracticestomanagerisksystematically. Common standard frameworks are the 4C’s model, NIST SP 800-190, and the Kubernetes Hardening Guide from the National Security Agency [3, 28, 5]. The 4C’s model stand for Cloud,Clusters,Containers, andCode. This framework aims to build a security process for these layers. For the Cloud layer, it focuses on infrastructure in Kubernetes, such as the control plane, nodes, etcd, and the API. Moreover, the security of the cloud provider is also a concern. The Cluster layer works on cluster components and the applications running. This layer has a list of areas including authentication, RBAC, network policies, secrets management, and Pod security standards. The Container layer is generally about container and OSvulnerabilities, containerruntimeisolation, imagesigning andenforcement,and disallowing unprivileged users. The final layer is the Code layer. However, this is out of scope for Kubernetes [3]. TheNISTSP800-190documentprimarilyworksforapplicationcontainertechnologies. Itraisesalistofmajorrisksforthecorecomponentsandsuggestscountermeasures fortherisks. Unfortunately,thisframeworkhasalimitedscopethatonlyworksforthe containers running inside the Kubernetes cluster [28]. TheKubernetesHardeningGuidedocumentisasecuritydocumentforadministratorsanddevelopersofUnitedStatesnationalsecuritysystems. Itcoverssecurity issues associated with improper configuration [5]. All the frameworks and guides abovearevaluableforuseinrealcloudsystems. Theyhelptoreducechallengesfor the complex nature of Kubernetes and document the best practices at the moment. The Kubernetes team also provides a listofsecurity practices thatfocus on control plane protection, secrets, workload protection, admission control, and auditing [3]. 2.4.4 Threat Matrix for Kubernetes MITRE ATT&CK is a global knowledge base of adversary strategies derived from applicationobservations. The ATT&CK isemployedasabasisfordesigningthreat modelsandmethodologiesfordiverseorganizations. [29]. Therearecurrently211 techniqueswith468sub-techniquesintheEnterprise ATT&CK matrixatthetimethis thesis is conducted, and MITRE continuously adds more. text: 3 Literature Review ThischapterprovidesaliteraturereviewonKubernetesclustersecuritybestpractices. Theliteraturereviewisstructuredtoanalyzethebestpracticesindiverselayersand components. Section 3.1 explores approaches for protecting a typical Kubernetes cluster. Section 3.2 delves deeper into safeguarding the workload and pod, which represent the core of the application. In the lower layer of workload, section 3.3 covers techniques to make Docker images and containerized applications safe for usage. Section 3.4 displays how tomonitor and what to monitor for eachcomponent and each layer. Section 3.5 focuses on compliance checks and methods to protect components that operate the system, such as control plane components and worker node components. Finally, section 3.6 represents the current gap of these practices and what is missing for a team to apply in software development. 3.1 Cluster Security At the highest cluster level, certain practices can influence the entire cluster. This sectionoutlines variouspracticesthatallcontributeto creatingasecurecluster. These practices include secret management, authentication, authorization, and managing namespace resource quotas. Typically, these tasks fall under the responsibilities of the cluster administrator. 3.1.1 Namespace Boundaries Kubernetesnamespacesaremeanttoseparateresourcesformultipleteamswithina cluster. Thereare3defaultnamespaceincluding kube-system,kube-public,default. Whilekube-systemandkube-publicnamespacescannotbedeleted,theyshouldnotbe utilizedtoorganizeresources. Customnamespacesprovideaninitiallayerofisolation betweenresources,especiallyusefulinmulti-tenantenvironments. Customnamespaces canfacilitateresourceisolation,effectivelyestablishingavirtualsegmentwithspecific rules, resource usage, and networktraffic controls. Additionally, developers can setup RBAC, resource limits, and more advanced configurations to enhance security [5]. However, Kubernetes Operators need privileges and may connect to resources across differentnamespaces[31]. Andongetal. identifiedvulnerabilitiesincross-namespace referenceswithinKubernetesOperatorduetodissimilaritybetweenthedeclaredscope of a resource and the actual scope of its process logic [31]. The same research shows that 317 out of 2268 Operators on GitHub are vulnerable to cross-namespace attacks. Consequently, lateral movement is not prevented by default between namespaces without advanced configuration. 3.1.2 Secrets Management CoderunningintheKubernetesclustergenerallyretrievessecretdata. text: 3.1.7 Dynamic Admission Control DynamicAdmissionControlisacomponentinKubernetesthatinterceptsrequests to the API server. It has an advanced function to allow customized configurations without impact on the kube-apiserver [3]. Dynamic admission control, implemented via admission webhooks, significantly strengthens Kubernetes security by providing a flexible andproactive mechanismfor enforcingcustom securitypoliciesat thepoint ofresourcecreationormodification. Itcansafeguardclusterresourcesbothduring runtimeandatdeployment. Intheruntimephase,theadmissioncontrollerprevents unauthorized access and configuration changes. Unlike built-in controllers, it can becustomizedtoaddressspecificsecuritythreats[42]. Duringpoddeployment,the dynamic admission controller can reject any deployments that violate established policies. It can be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline, aligning with modern software development processes and integrating with image scanning tools to prevent the deployment of vulnerable images [43]. 3.2 Workload and Pod Level Security Workloads or pods are the most fine-grained means to run the main business logic of the application. Therefore, they should be taken care of. This subsection discusses security issues related to configuration to protect a single workload running in the cluster. 3.2.1 Pod Security Standards and Pod Security Admission Securing configurations in Kubernetes workload presents significant challenges. An improperly configured pod may be vulnerable to exploitation, which could potentially compromisetheintegrityoftheentirecluster. PodSecurityStandards(PSSs)makethe security configuration painless and efficient. PSSs are Kubernetes policies for pods to ensure that all pods in the cluster adhere to consistent security baselines, minimizing vulnerabilitieswhilemaintainingoperationalflexibilitywithdifferentlevels. ThePSA enforces these policies. Three levels of Pod Security Standards are: •Privileged: Openpolicyprovidingthewidestlevelofpermissions. Itpermits some privilege escalations. [3]. •Baseline: Policythatminimallyrestrictsandcontrolssomeprivilegeescalations. It permits the default configuration for Pods with minimal specifications. [3]. •Restricted: Themostrestrictedone,usinghardeningbestpracticesforPods[3]. Pod Security Admission (PSA) is a built-in admission controller embedded in the kube-apiserver that enforces the PSS. If a request violates the policies defined in the PSS, PSA will react in 3 different modes: •enforce: Policy violations result in the rejection of the pod. [3].

title: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes
text: •audit: Policy violations lead to an audit annotation being added to the event recorded in the audit log, while they are still permitted. [3]. •warn: Policy violations result in a warning for the user. However, they are generally permitted. [3]. PSS and PSA work together as a two-part system. PSS provides the policy framework, while the PSA module operates it through namespace labels and real-time validationofpodcreationandupdateperiods. ThisduoisKubernetes’primarypod securitymechanism,ensuringclustersaresecure,flexible,andeasytomanage. An admin labels a namespace with a PSS level. When a pod is created, PSA reads the namespace label, fetches the corresponding PSS policy, and validates the pod’s spec. Depending on the configured policies and PSA mode set, PSA may reject, log data, or trigger an alert. 3.2.2 Deploy Honeypot Workload and Intrusion Detection Systems Canary deployment is a strategy to deploy multiple versions of an application and routeasmallamountoftrafficfromasmallusersubsettothenewpodversionsbefore fully rolling out. This technique can be applied in the Kubernetes environment. Fake workloads and services are deployed in the cluster. These resources act as a honeypot and are intentionally exposed to vulnerabilities in the cluster to monitor and notify the team of intrusions and attackers. Attackers can not differentiate between real and fake ones. Anomalous patterns will be monitored and detected by IDS [25]. After the attack actions are detected, the attack is traced back to the initial vector and alerted to the team [25]. The setup for this approach is presented in Figure 5. When an attacker sendsmalicioustraffictotheKubernetescluster,theyarelikelytotargetthevulnerable pod,whichisactuallythehoneypot. Thisdivertstheattackerawayfromtheprivileged pod, which contains the actual sensitive data or critical workloads. OtherstudiesshowthathoneypotisaneffectiveweaponinKubernetessecurity. For example, Noah et al. built a honey pod system using Docker, Kubernetes, and workflow tools such as Apache Airflow, Spinnaker, and Argo for 3 months to uncover the risk associated with a Kubernetes system [44]. They received 191 attacks and categorized4maintypesofattackontheircluster,includingexploitingtheexposed service, establishing persistence on the hijacked system, cryptocurrency mining, checking for updates, or continuing to spread. In detail, the cluster was attacked by 3 big groups and others by diverse methods. The activities on containers are installing a rootkit,containerescape,establishingpersistence,disablinghostdefenses,installing crypto-miners and launching an unprivileged container. In the Kubernetes layers, they initially exploited the API server, kubelet, and the exposed endpoints. text: 4 Methodology The primary goal for a new method is to provide a comprehensive approach for Kubernetessecurity. Section 4.1 discusses the overview of a new method. Section 4.2 explores the details of the main methods and displays their structure. Section 4.3 finally shows how it can be applied by developers in a real system. 4.1 Methodology Overview Some Kubernetes security frameworks have been introduced in Security Frameworks, includingthe4C’sModel,NISTSP800-190,andKubernetesHardeningGuidance. However,theseframeworksjustgivealistofbestpracticesandrecommendations,and it is still far away from a comprehensive approach with fine-grained instructions for a Kubernetessystem. Thissectionintroducesanewapproachforusingmatrix-based Kubernetes security. An initial version of this matrix security is shown in Table 3. Kubernetes clusters and Docker containers are both included in the scope of this new approach, which is a difference from the frameworks introduced earlier. The scope islimitedonthesetechnologiesbecausetheyarethestandardindustrialonesatthe moment. There are other alternatives used in the market for containerisation and orchestration,suchasDockerSwarmandOpenShift. However,duetothedifference inthenatureoftechnology,thismethodisnotguaranteedtobeappliedinthosecases. Another difference with other approaches is that this matrix is not just a siloed guidancewithalistofpractices. Thematrixisinspiredmainlybyusingideasfrom the shift-left security anddefense-in-depth principles introduced in section Security Frameworks. Martinetal. recommendimplementingadefense-in-depthstrategyfor software applications [71]. Consequently, this approach is an effective wayto defend different layers of Kubernetes systems. Research by Zlatan et al. has the same idea to conductdefense indepthindifferent layersas amainstrategy tosecureKubernetes. They split the layers into components into control plane, node security, image and workload, access management, observability and auditing [72]. In contrast, this thesis work divides layers by architectural structure of a cluster from the container to the pod and cluster-wide level. It is crucial to apply the methodology across different layers cause Garsha claimed that Kubernetes has a layered architecture [35]. Each layerhasitsownsecurityrequirementsandsettings. Thislayeredarchitecturerequires defenseindepth,whichisbotheffectiveanddesirable,aslongaseachlayerisproperly secured [35]. By using a 2D matrix-based approach, it can be ensured to cover securitypractices ateachstageofthedevelopmentprocess. Thismatrixsupportsboth developers and cluster administrators to keep track of the security status for the whole cluster. It simplifies the complex structure of Kubernetes and its attack vector. That’s why this is more comprehensive and better than other frameworks.

In this layer, security is assured by applying CIS Benchmarks, a set of configurations. WorkloadLayerincludespracticesthatworkforagroupofapplicationsrunning business logic inside pods. The main practices in this layer harden the pods and applicationswithstaticscanningtodetectvulnerabilities. Inaddition,theconfiguration covers resource management. The highest layer, which is the Cluster Layer, comprises protections for the whole system at the cluster range and configuration impact on different namespaces. The practices for this layer such as RBAC, secrets management, and namespace resource quotas, have the highest impact scope. 4.2.2 Stage Definition The proposed matrix is inspired by the DevOps life cycle at 2.3 and organizes Kubernetes security practices into three core stages. These stages provide a structured approachtosecuringKubernetesenvironmentswhileallowingflexibilityforadaptation based on project-specific workflows. •Build stage: software is designed with requirements and developed. •Deployment Stage: software is built into the artifact and deployed. •Runtime Stage: container and cluster are running and maintained in the cluster. Whilethese3stagesprovidefunctionalstructure,thedevelopmentteammayadjust the matrix to fix their need. There are possible added stages for a workflow: •Pre-Build Stage: A team can integrate preliminary steps for security, such as threatmodellingandsecurityplanning,anddocumentpreparationforsuitable cloud providers. •Sub-stages before release: Before the final release, there can be multiple stages for testing in a pre-production environment. In this stage, practices can be applied to validate network policies and fine-tune RBAC rules before releasing to production. •Termination Stage: A cluster can include many jobs and cron jobs that request persistent volumes during task running. This supports cleaning up processes to clear data remains and frees up space. These variations accommodate diverse workflows, such as legacy system integrations or multi-cloud deployments, without compromising security. While DevSecOps pipelines automate security across the software development lifecycle, this framework differs in scope and flexibility. This matrix allows dev teams to choose solutions, security tools, and implementations that best fit their environment.

title: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes
text: 4.2.3 Item Definition The proposed Kubernetes security matrix provides a systematic framework for addressing risks across the entire lifecycle of a Kubernetes environment. Each cell in the matrix contains multiple security measures designed for specific Kubernetes components, such as the control plane, pods/workloads, or containers, at a defined stage of development. Arrangement of items is presented in Table 3. Each item in the matrix is described in detail in section 3. The item reference would describe in detail instructions on how to conduct the practice. This approach not only providesahigh-levelblueprintbutalsoprovidesdetailedinstructionstoimplementthe configuration. In short, the matrix provides a high-level, adaptable blueprint for securing Kubernetes. Each cell references detailed instructions for engineers, such as configuring Pod Security Admission (PSA) for Workloads, integrating Trivy into image scanning, etc. Thisend-to-endmitigationdemonstrateshowthematrixensuresrisksarefully addressed. 4.3 How to Apply When Building And Releasing Software The matrix serves as both a strategic guide and a practical checklist for securing Kubernetes environments. By aligning security practices with software development stagesandarchitecturallayers,developerteamscansystematicallymitigateriskswhile maintaining agility. However, it can be challenging to apply this approach to a real software engineer’sstandardprocess. In real-life scenarios, developerteamsworkin a linear order with tasks followed by the next tasks. There must be a method to convert the 2D approach into a linear order of configuration. This can possibly be done by applying the two methods below: layer by layer and stage by stage. 4.3.1 Layer By Layer Inalayer-by-layerimplementation,secureconfigurationsareappliedandtestedfor each layer. In a simple term, the security practices are conducted from the bottom to the upper layers. First, an application with multiple services and clusters can be built, deployed, and run. Second, image and container hardening are involved to ensure security at the first layer for containers. Then, configuration for middle layers, which are components in the worker nodes, pods, and the control plane, is implemented. Finally, the system is tested with practice from the top layer with a configuration correlated to the whole cluster’s impacts. It is not mandatory to configure layers in the above order. Each layer target is isolated,andtheconfigurationineachlayerdoesnotimpacttheothers. However,after each layer configuration, to make sure the functional requirements of the project work rationallywithoutbrokenfeatures,thewholeapplicationflowshouldbeend-to-end tested. This method requires many iterations of configuration and testing, but it is morefriendlyadaptedandconducted. Anyinterruptedsettingupcouldbeeasilyfound and fixed.

title: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes
text: 4.3.2 Stage By Stage Different from the layer-by-layer implementation, the stage-by-stage method requires teams to come up with the configuration across the whole cluster. This is slightly similar to the Waterfall Model, a traditional software development method introduced in 1970. All requirements and configurations are analyzed and defined carefully. This makes the matrix implementation seem to be short for a project, but the workload for each stage is enormous, especially the Development Stage. Moreover, it is extremely complicated to troubleshoot errors. 4.3.3 Agile Implementation Compare the two implementations above; each has its pros and cons. In modern software development philosophy, iterative and incremental approaches are foundational. Iterative elements allow the developer team to refine features based on feedback, and incremental elements ensure stable progress. Incremental principles support the team in releasing the features quickly. It is now common for organizations to release prototypes or minimum viable products (MVPs) rapidlytogatherfeedback. Fromthefeedback,featuresareupdated,upgraded,and addediteratively. CombinedwithDevOps,newfeaturesarecontinuouslyintegrated and delivered (CI/CD). To adapt to modern software development methodologies and tomakeexpandingthesoftwarewithnewfeaturessmooth,aniterativeapproach,which islayerbylayer,canbeappliedtotheproposedmatrix. Asimplewaytoexecutethe matrix for an initial insecure cluster is presented in section 4.3.1. Furthermore, when new features or services are added later, multiple iterations, which means multiple layers, can be applied to the new features. This makes the product delivered smoothly to end users without much interruption to the experience. This approach will be performed in the next section with a real case study implementation.

Section 5.4 shows configurations for Pod, which include higher-level workloads such as StatefulSet, Job andDeployment. Finally,section5.5examinespracticesforcluster-wideconfiguration. 5.1 Application Overview Anonymous Registration Anonymous Access Protocol (ARA2) is a distributed authenticationsystemdesignedforKubernetesenvironments. Thissystemisacryptographic flowthatallowsuserstoauthenticateusingcryptographictokenexchangesbetween decentralisedcomponentsandthenaccessprotectedresources. TheARA2application serves as an ideal examination for the security matrix due to its multi-component architecture, sensitive data and complex multiple operations. This system consists of the main custom services: Dealer,Guard,Access,User,Wikiand 2 dependencies: Strimzi Kafka Operator andMongoDB. Figure 6 show the architecture of the case study system. All services in ARA2 are deployed across multiple instancesto create a distributed environment. All user requests are HTTP-based, and all connections between services in the cluster are made through the Strimzi Kafka Operator. The general workflow of the system can be simplified into three main processes: •System setup: Dealers generate a token and distribute it to Guards. Guards then generate a token from the received data.

text: •User Authentication: User identifies using the generated token to the Dealer via the User proxy. •User Authorisation: User accesses through the Wiki service using a user token andanaccesskey. Thecombination issenttotheGuardtovalidatepermissions 5.1.1 Dealer The Dealer service is the first crucial component in the protocol. It provides the access key token to the user. Given the requirement of anonymous and distributed authentication,theDealermustremainaseparatecomponent,ensuringitcannotobtain more information than necessary to provide the access key. Additionally, since the Dealer’sdegreemustremainsecret,itoperatesasamulti-instancecomponent, with each instance holding its own degree. The Dealer’s main purpose in the setup stage is to generate its own polynomial, partition it into N shares and send it to the Guard. Duringtheauthenticationstage,theDealermustverifytheidentificationcredentials and reply with its token. The life cycle of a Dealer comprises the following steps: •Connect to the Kafka broker •Subscribe to its topic •Request for the topics of the Guards from the Kafka broker •Check for the existence of a state file (which indicates a previously aborted execution). •If the state file does not include the value of its polynomial or the state file does not exist, generate its own polynomial and save it in the state file. •If the state file does not indicate that the token was already sent to the Guards, partition the token and send it to the Guards. •Continuously formessages from the AuthManager, including the user’s identification, and process the messages accordingly 5.1.2 Guard TheGuardcomponentiscrucialtotheimplementationoftheprotocolasitverifies user accessto the resource. Itfunctions as the Guardin the original protocol. Due to the requirement for anonymous and distributed authentication, the Guard must remain aseparatecomponent. Itshouldnotacquiremoreinformationthannecessarytoverify userauthorization. Additionally,sincetheGuard’sowncomputeddegreemustremain secret,itisamulti-instancecomponent,witheachinstancemaintainingitsowndegree. The Guard’s two main functions are exchanging messages with Dealers during the setupphasetocreateitsownkeyandusethatkeytoverifyusers’requeststoresources. The life cycle of a Guard consists of the following steps:

text: 6 Discussion Thissectiondiscussestheeffectivenessofthepreviouscasestudyworkandlimitations for future exploration. Section 6.1 will cover how the new approach works. In section 6.2, challenges during the implementation of the case study are illustrated. Finally, section 6.3 willshow the limitations of thecurrent approach, and this leads tofuture open research questions on how to improve this new approach. 6.1 Case Study Output Result Fromthecasestudysection,theimplementationofmatrix-basedsecuritydemonstrated significanteffectivenessinhardeningtheARA2Kubernetessystem. Bysystematically applying layered security practices aligned with development stages, critical vulnerabilitiesweresubstantiallyreduced. Mostimpactfully,theadoptionofdistrolessimages eliminated 100% of critical and high-severity vulnerabilities across all main services and reduced the workload misconfiguration by 52%. The CIS Benchmark automation via Microk8s further validated cluster-wide configuration and reduced configuration failures from 25 to 0. All these quantitative gains confirm the framework’s capacity to mitigate risks for a case study cluster. Qualitatively, the matrix layered approach provided clear prioritization, while iterative per-layer testing prevented systemic failures. However, the dependency on third-party Operators complicated cluster-wide policies, revealing ecosystem integration as a key challenge. The shift-left integration of practices like development stage image scanning also proved valuable for early vulnerability detection. On the other hand, the most important discussion is the trade-off between security and availability during implementation. PSS restricted mode and recommended configurationcausethebreakingoffunctionalities. Similarly,default-denynetwork policies disrupted Kafka Operator communications due to inconsistent labeling conventions. This highlights the need for iterative experimentation to find out the optimal balance points with maximal security without functional corruption. In this work, the balance point is discovered for network policies and Pod configuration, but this point requires team effort and time investment. 6.2 Challenges Implementing the proposed Kubernetes security framework presented several significant challenges that highlight the gap between theoretical models and practical deployment. First, a steep learning curve is an issue for the teams. There are many challengingissues,suchasnetworkpoliciesrequiringextensivedocumentationand testingwithoutaproper,friendlytool. Moreover,itisnoteasytoexactlyfollowthe best practice and make the application operate correctly. Practices such as denying allnetworkpoliciesdonotworkinthecasestudy,causingtheintegrationofuncontrolled Kubernetes Operators in the cluster. Operator Pods’ labels that are not well configuredmakeittoughforteamstoapply. Inaddition,eachpracticeisdefinedin

text: the development life cycle, which requires huge effort for researching, integration, configuration,validation,anddocumentation. Toovercomethesechallenges,teams need competency experts, which is a high cost for the organization. Comprehensivetestingacrosslayers/stagescreatedexponentialvalidationoverhead. Writing a unit and functional test case can be time-consuming. To cover different componentsoftheclusterinaworkflow,End-to-end(e2e)testingcanbeasolution. E2etestingaddressesthechallengeoftestinglargeintegratedsystemsandvalidates the whole workflow, but it may slow down the execution time [77]. It creates a complex test environment setup and test flakiness. These issues can lead to increased maintenance costs, slower feedback loops, and difficulty in debugging failures. In modern software development, a cross-functional team setup is popular. To work together in the teams, tasks are assigned to members during the planning stage. A challenge of using this matrix is how configuration tasks can be distributed to team members to balance the coding and configuration sides withoutoverheadand technical debt. Additionally, putting the matrix into use requires a cost in meeting hours increment, aligning the results, and learning and development. Moreover, in the contextofAgileandScrumsoftwaredevelopment,thisrequiresclosercollaboration between the Product Owner, the Scrum Master, and the Development Team. The duty ofplanninganddividingtasksisassignedtotheDevelopmentTeam,andthisraises more costs for the organization. The final challenge in this work is execution and adaptation on different cloud providers’services. Therearemanycloudprovidersinthemarket,withthebiggest ones being Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform, and the thesis aims to cover a provider-agnostic solution. To adapt to a detailed cloudenvironment,actionsarerequiredinresearchingandcustomization. Eventhe high-level details provide a comprehensive structure, efforts are demanded to transfer fromtheorytoapplicationonaspecificcloudprovideroron-premiseenvironment,and requiremorevolumeofwork. Thisworkdoesnotdeliverinstructionsformigration between different cloud systems. Thesechallengesvalidatetheframework’snovelty,whileexistingsolutionsaddress isolated Kubernetes risks, this matrix approach uniquely simplifies and exposes systemicgapsinenterpriseadoption. Futureworkwilldevelopabstractioninstructions to simplify environment-specific adaptations. 6.3 Limitation and Future Work Whiletheproposedsecurityframeworkandthecasestudydemonstratethefeasibilityof Kuberneteshardening,severallimitationsmeritconsiderationforfurtheradvancement. Thissectionstateslimitationsinthisthesisworkandprovidesfutureresearchdirections toimprovethese limitations toprovidethemostcompleteguidelineforthesoftware industry. Initially, the validation was constrained to small-scale clusters (1 control plane and 1 worker node), limiting insights into enterprise-grade environments. Using this simple environmental setup can’t sufficiently demonstrate the distributed nature of Kubernetes and microservices, and can’t show the power of resource management

There should be a future investigationtofindthecorrectbalancepointbetweensecurityanddevelopmentspeed for teams. To strongly prove the theory and gather more accurate data, the case study should be conducted in a more complex system and a bigger environment cluster with multiple nodes. Second, due to the limitedcapacityofthe thesis works, notallthe practices are applied,whichmakesitunsatisfactorytoprovethetheory. This is a minor loss in the work, and there should bemorepracticeappliedtocoverthewholematrixdesign. title: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes
text: 7 Conclusion Kubernetesisadefactotechnologyforbuildingscalable,resilient,andhighlyavailable systems. This also introduces critical security challenges for team members who interact with the cluster. These challenges come from the complex architecture of the Kubernetes cluster, which requires multiple practices and research, including misconfigurationanalysis, secretsprotection,runtimebehaviormonitoring, andsoon. While existing guidelines, CIS Benchmark and NSA Hardening Guide, offer the best practices,theyarefarfromamanageablesystematicapproachthatcouldbeintegrated with the modern DevOps life cycle. Moreover, they still leave many attack vectors uncovered. This thesis work addresses the gap by introducing a robust and systematic approachasa2-dimensionalmatrix. Itdividesthearchitecturaldesignintocontainers, workload, control plane, and worker node components, and finally the whole cluster scope with 3 main stages shortlisted from the DevOps stages, including development, deployment, and runtime. The new approach innovation comes from the combination of defense-in-depth and shift-left security principles. During the thesis case study implementation. Practices such as distroless containerization, image scanning, CIS automation via Microk8s,andnetworkpoliciesareenforcedintolayersatdifferentstagestoensure vulnerabilitiesaredetected,mitigated,andfixedsystematically. Thecasestudywas validatedonARA2,ananonymousauthenticationsystem,wheretheresultreached 100%eliminationofcriticalandhighseverityvulnerabilitiesinthecorecontainers. Despite challenges in fixing security recommendations, the iterative layer-by-layer implementationsuccessfullydeliveredahealthycluster, whichprovedtheadaptability of modern Agile and DevOps flows into the hardening process. This later flexibly extends the development, security of new features and microservices added to the cluster. Even though there are successes in the thesis work, there are limitations that remain. The case study was conducted on a single cluster with a control plane and a worker node only. Due to limited capacity, the work lacks efficient integration of runtime practices would give more insight about the runtime threats and generate more ideas to improve the current operation. All the limitations make the future work critical to extend the case study scale to match the nature of a distributed system, and perfectizetheimplementationofthewholematrixprocess. Academically,thereare challenges pointedout during the implementation thatraise many researchquestions about finding a balancing point of security and functionality, and also the question of security practice missing for third-party Operators running inside the cluster. In conclusion, this thesis redefines Kubernetes processes, making fragmented guidelinesintoaunified,lifecycle-integratedandsystematicparadigm. Inthenear future,Kubernetesmayevolve,andthismatrix-basedstrategyisstillafoundational blueprint that can better adapt to new architectures and adjustments.
